title: Why Efficiency Matters & Mixed-Precision Training
content_md:
Modern LLMs are enormous, and the cost of running them dominates real-world deployment. A single LLM text generation is roughly **150x more expensive than a Google search**, and the chief driver of inference cost is the **number of tokens generated** — every step has to push the full set of parameters through the GPU. Model size and architecture matter too, but if you halve generated length, you almost halve cost.

The **scaling laws** tell us loss falls as a *power law* in the number of parameters, but with strongly diminishing returns. For a fixed compute budget there is an *optimal* model size — bigger is not automatically better. This motivates the entire efficiency toolkit: **quantization, KV-caching, MoE, and distillation** all aim to deliver the most capability per FLOP and per byte of VRAM.

A second pressure point is **training memory**. Each parameter normally needs (a) the weight itself, (b) the gradient, and (c) optimizer state (Adam keeps two moments). In fp32, that's 16 bytes/parameter — a 7B model already needs ~112 GB just for state. **Mixed-precision training** is the standard fix.

The recipe: keep a **master copy of weights in fp32**, but during the forward and backward passes use **fp16 (or bf16) working copies**. Multiplications in fp16 are ~2x faster on tensor cores and use half the memory. The problem is that fp16's tiny dynamic range can underflow gradients to zero. So the loss is **scaled up** before backprop (so small gradients land in fp16's representable range), and gradients are **rescaled back to fp32** before the optimizer update touches the master weights.

**bf16** (brain-float) keeps fp32's 8 exponent bits but drops mantissa precision; this trades precision for range and avoids most of the underflow drama at the cost of slightly noisier weights. fp16 has more precision but smaller range, hence the loss-scaling trick.

Why bother with the master fp32 copy? Repeated tiny updates ($w \leftarrow w - \eta g$) lose information when $\eta g$ is below the fp16 ULP — the update becomes a no-op. Keeping the master in fp32 preserves these small updates, while the fp16 working copy gets you the speed.

The theme to remember: **efficiency techniques exploit redundancy.** Networks are over-parameterized, so we can quantize weights, share heads, route to subsets, or distill into smaller students — all with limited accuracy loss. The rest of this lecture is a tour of those four levers.

checks.q: What is the dominant factor in LLM inference cost?

options.text: Vocabulary size
Number of tokens generated
Number of attention heads
Tokenizer choice

checks.explanation: Each generation step pushes the whole model through the GPU, so total cost scales primarily with how many tokens you produce.

checks.q: In mixed-precision training, why is a master copy of the weights kept in fp32?

options.text: fp16 cannot represent negative numbers
GPU tensor cores only accept fp32 inputs
Tiny weight updates would underflow in fp16 and be lost
fp32 weights take less memory than fp16

checks.explanation: Updates of size eta*g often fall below fp16's ULP. Keeping a master fp32 copy preserves them; fp16 working copies provide the speed.

checks.q: Why are gradients rescaled before being applied to the fp32 master weights?

options.text: To compensate for the loss-scaling factor used to keep gradients in fp16's range
To enforce sparsity on the gradient vector
To convert gradients into integer form
To match the dimensionality of the optimizer state

checks.explanation: We multiply the loss up so small gradients survive in fp16, then divide them back down in fp32 before the update — otherwise the optimizer step is biased.

title: Quantization: Asymmetric Quant and the Outlier Trap
content_md:
**Quantization** trades numerical precision for memory and speed: store weights (and sometimes activations) in low-bit integers like int8 instead of fp16/fp32. Because deep nets are heavily over-parameterized (the same intuition behind LoRA's low-rank claim), small precision losses don't usually move the needle on accuracy.

The most common method is **asymmetric quantization**, which linearly maps the float range $[\min(X), \max(X)]$ onto a signed $b$-bit integer range. Two parameters fully describe the mapping — a **scale** $s$ and a **zero-point** $z$:

$$s = \frac{2^b - 1}{\max(X) - \min(X)}, \quad z = -\text{round}(\min(X)\cdot s) - 2^{b-1}$$

$$X_{quant} = \text{round}(X\cdot s + z), \quad X_{dequant} = (X_{quant} - z)/s$$

The scale stretches the range to fill the integer interval; the zero-point shifts it so that the float 0 lands on an exact integer (important for ReLU, padding, masking).

**Worked example (slide 12).** A weight matrix has $\min = -9.553$, $\max = 7.929$, $b = 8$.
- $s = 255 / (7.929 - (-9.553)) = 14.586$
- $z = -\text{round}(-9.553 \cdot 14.586) - 128 = 11$

So a value like $0.5$ maps to $\text{round}(0.5\cdot 14.586) + 11 = 18$.

**The outlier trap.** Replace the smallest entry $-9.553$ with an outlier $-999.999$. Now the range explodes:
- $s \approx 0.253$
- Almost every "normal" value (everything between roughly $-1$ and $+8$) ends up rounded to the same integer (e.g., 125). You've **collapsed dozens of distinct floats onto one int** — catastrophic information loss.

This is the canonical PTQ failure mode and is why **clipping with calibration** is standard practice. You either pick clip thresholds with a static dataset before deployment, or compute them dynamically per layer from running activation statistics. In the example above, $\text{clip}(X, -10, 10)$ rescues the mapping.

**PTQ vs QAT vs QT.** **Post-Training Quantization** (PTQ) is fast and common — quantize a finished model, possibly with a brief calibration pass — but suffers most degradation. **Quantization-Aware Training** (QAT) simulates quantization in the forward pass while still backpropagating in full precision, letting the model adapt. **Quantized Training** (QT) keeps everything in low precision but is rare and brittle.

**Straight-Through Estimator (STE).** QAT has a calculus problem: $\text{round}(\cdot)$ has zero (or undefined) gradient almost everywhere. STE fixes this by *pretending* the (de)quantizer is the identity inside the clipping range — gradient $= 1$ inside, $0$ outside. The forward pass still uses the real round, but gradients flow as if it weren't there. STE is exactly the trick that lets gradient descent see through a non-differentiable bottleneck.

Key takeaways: asymmetric quant is two numbers ($s$, $z$); outliers blow up $s$ and crush dynamic range; clipping is the fix; STE is what makes QAT trainable.

checks.q: Why does a single outlier value catastrophically degrade asymmetric quantization?

options.text: It causes integer overflow in the zero-point
It enlarges max - min, shrinking the scale s and collapsing many distinct floats onto the same integer
It forces the use of fp32 throughout the network
It changes the sign of the zero-point

checks.explanation: A huge value blows up the float range, so s = (2^b - 1)/range becomes tiny. Nearby normal values round to the same int — massive information loss.

checks.q: What is the role of the zero-point z in asymmetric quantization?

options.text: It sets the learning rate during quantization-aware training
It shifts the integer mapping so that the float 0 corresponds to an exact integer
It selects which layers get quantized
It is the bias added to the loss function

checks.explanation: The zero-point is the integer that float 0 maps to, ensuring 0 is exactly representable — important for ReLU, masks, and padding.

checks.q: Why does QAT need the Straight-Through Estimator?

options.text: Because softmax is non-monotonic
Because round() has zero or undefined gradient, so backprop cannot flow through it without help
Because int8 multiplications are non-associative
Because the loss must be quantized too

checks.explanation: round() has gradient 0 (or undefined) almost everywhere. STE pretends the quantizer is the identity inside the clip range so gradients can flow.

title: KV-Cache: Trading Memory for Inference Speed
content_md:
Autoregressive decoding has a redundancy problem. At step $t$, attention recomputes $K = XW_K$ and $V = XW_V$ over the **entire prefix** even though those rows were already computed at steps $1, 2, \ldots, t-1$ — only the new token's row is fresh. The fix is the **KV cache**: store the previous $K$ and $V$ matrices in VRAM, and at each step append only the new token's row.

With a KV cache, the per-step computation collapses dramatically. At step $t = 7$ of generating a sentence, the query $Q$ is only the **single new row** ($1 \times 512$ instead of $6 \times 512$), and we attend it against the cached $K, V$. Compute drops from quadratic-in-prefix to linear-in-prefix per step — a huge win.

The price: **VRAM**. The cache holds $K$ and $V$ for every past token at every layer for every head. The formula is

$$\text{VRAM} = 2 \cdot p \cdot b \cdot n \cdot h \cdot d \cdot t$$

where $2$ accounts for both $K$ and $V$, $p$ is bytes/element, $b$ batch size, $n$ layers, $h$ heads, $d$ head dim, $t$ sequence length.

**Worked example: LLaMA-7B (slide 30).** With $p=2$ (fp16), $b=32$, $n=32$, $h=32$, $d=128$, $t=500$:

$\text{VRAM} = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 32 \cdot 32 \cdot 32 \cdot 128 \cdot 500 \approx 7.82\,\text{GB}$

that's *on top of* the ~14–16 GB the weights themselves occupy. So the cache is non-negligible — and it grows linearly with both $t$ and $b$.

**Knobs to shrink the cache.** You can use a smaller model (cuts $n, h, d$), smaller batch ($b$), shorter context ($t$), or lower precision ($p$). Beyond that there are **attention architecture variants**:

- **Sliding-window attention.** Attend only to the last $k$ tokens. Cache size becomes $O(k)$ instead of $O(t)$.
- **Multi-Query Attention (MQA).** All query heads share **one** $K/V$ head. The cache shrinks by a factor of $1/h$. Note: $d = d_{model}/h$, so per-head dim grows when $h$ shrinks; the *compute* roughly balances, but the *cache* is genuinely smaller.
- **Grouped-Query Attention (GQA).** A middle ground: $m$ shared $K/V$ heads serve $m$ groups of query heads. Trades quality vs cache more smoothly than MQA.
- **Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA, DeepSeek).** Project $K, V$ down to a low-rank latent $r \ll d$ via $W^{down} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$, cache only the latent, then up-project with $W^{up}$ at attention time. This reuses LoRA's low-rank intuition specifically for the cache.

**The crucial trap to internalize:** the KV cache **does not save compute relative to a hypothetical no-cache baseline that recomputes from scratch** — it saves compute *because* it caches. It is a memory-vs-compute tradeoff. If you're VRAM-bound, you may need to give some of that compute back via shorter contexts, smaller batch, MQA/GQA/MLA, or by quantizing the cache itself to int8.

checks.q: What is the primary tradeoff introduced by a KV cache?

options.text: Saves compute but costs additional VRAM
Saves VRAM but costs more compute
Saves both compute and VRAM at no cost
Improves accuracy at the cost of latency

checks.explanation: By storing K and V from previous steps you skip recomputing them, but those matrices live in VRAM and grow linearly with t and b.

checks.q: Multi-Query Attention reduces KV-cache size by approximately what factor (with h heads)?

options.text: 1/log(h)
1/sqrt(h)

checks.explanation: MQA shares a single K/V head across all h query heads, so the cache shrinks by 1/h.

checks.q: For LLaMA-7B with p=2, b=32, n=32, h=32, d=128, t=500, the KV-cache VRAM is closest to:

options.text: ~78 GB

checks.explanation: VRAM = 2*2*32*32*32*128*500 ≈ 7.82 GB, on top of weight storage.

title: Mixture of Experts: Sparse Routing and Load Balancing
content_md:
**Mixture of Experts (MoE)** replaces a transformer block (typically the FFN) with $n$ parallel **expert** subnetworks plus a **gate** that decides which experts to run. The output is a weighted sum:

$$y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} G(x)_i\, E_i(x)$$

The gate $G$ produces a probability distribution over experts; the router uses those probabilities to pick experts.

**Dense MoE.** Every expert runs every step; output is the soft-weighted sum. This is easy to train but **gives no efficiency gain** — you've added experts without saving any compute. Capacity goes up; FLOPs go up too.

**Sparse MoE (Top-k).** Only the top-$k$ experts (commonly $k=2$) actually run; their probabilities are softmax-renormalized and the others contribute nothing. Now the model can have hundreds of experts, but each token only activates a small handful — capacity scales with $n$ while inference compute scales with $k$. The gating can be written as

$$G(x) = \text{Softmax}(\text{TopK}(W_{gate} x,\, k))$$

This is the trick behind models like Mixtral and Switch Transformer.

**The crucial trap to internalize.** All $n$ experts still **live in memory** even though only $k$ are *active per token*. So MoE saves **compute**, not VRAM. A 47B-parameter sparse MoE may activate only 13B per token but still requires VRAM for all 47B.

**Gate collapse.** Sparse gates love to over-pick a few prominent experts. The popular ones train fast, the unpopular ones never see data, and you collapse to a tiny effective ensemble. Mitigations:

- **Stochastic (noisy) routing.** Add tunable Gaussian noise to the gate logits:

$$H(x) = W_{gate} x + \mathcal{N}(0,1) \cdot \text{SoftPlus}(W_{noise} x)$$

SoftPlus is a smooth, always-positive ReLU substitute that scales the noise standard deviation per token. The noise gives all experts occasional chances — passive load balancing.

- **Load-balancing auxiliary loss.** Add

$$L_{aux} = w_{aux} \cdot N \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot P_i$$

where $f_i$ is the fraction of tokens routed to expert $i$ and $P_i$ is the average gate probability for expert $i$. The product is minimized when both are uniform — actively pressuring the gate to spread the load.

- **Entropy regularization** (encourage high-entropy gate distributions) and **soft routing** (use weighted top-$k$ rather than a hard pick) are softer alternatives.

**Why isn't the discrete top-k a backprop disaster?** It is theoretically discontinuous, but the original Shazeer et al. paper observed empirically that this isn't a real problem — gradients still flow through the chosen experts and through the softmax over the top-$k$ logits, which is enough to learn good routing.

The overall picture: MoE is a way to **decouple capacity from per-token compute**. You pay for it with (a) all-experts-in-memory VRAM, (b) extra training-time bookkeeping for load balance, and (c) sometimes flakier optimization.

checks.q: What is the key efficiency property of sparse Top-k MoE?

options.text: Only the active experts are stored in memory
All experts live in memory but only k are active per token, so compute (not VRAM) scales sub-linearly in n
The router replaces self-attention entirely
It eliminates the need for a softmax in the gate

checks.explanation: All n experts must be loaded; sparsity only saves compute by activating k of them per token.

checks.q: Why does Dense MoE provide essentially no efficiency gain?

options.text: It uses fp32 throughout
It cannot be parallelized across GPUs
All experts run for every input, so adding experts adds compute proportionally
Its gate is non-differentiable

checks.explanation: Dense MoE runs every expert every step. Capacity grows but so does compute — there is no sparsity to exploit.

checks.q: What does the load-balancing auxiliary loss L_aux = w*N*sum(f_i * P_i) discourage?

options.text: Long input sequences
Gate collapse onto a few popular experts
The use of softmax in the gate
Multi-head attention

checks.explanation: The product f_i * P_i is minimized when both fractions and probabilities are uniform — directly pushing the router to spread tokens across experts.

title: Knowledge Distillation: Teacher to Student
content_md:
**Knowledge distillation** compresses a big, accurate **teacher** model into a smaller, cheaper **student** model. The transfer always flows **teacher to student** — never the other way around. (A common exam trap: the smaller model is the student that *learns from* the teacher's outputs.)

Three flavors, in increasing order of richness and complexity:

**1. Response distillation.** The simplest. Run the teacher over a corpus, collect its predicted **hard labels**, and train the student on those labels with a normal cross-entropy loss. This is essentially "use the teacher as a free annotator." Pros: dead simple, no teacher needed at student-train time after labeling. Cons: hard labels throw away all of the teacher's *uncertainty* — if the teacher was 51%/49% torn between two classes, the student only sees the winner.

**2. Logits distillation (Hinton-style).** Match the **soft probability distributions**. Concretely, minimize the KL divergence between the teacher's and student's output distributions (often with a temperature $T > 1$ that softens both):

$$L_{KD} = \text{KL}(p_T(x) \,\|\, p_S(x))$$

Usually combined with the student's standard supervised loss (a weighted sum of $L_{KD}$ and $L_{CE}$). Soft labels carry **dark knowledge** — relative similarities between classes — that hard labels cannot. A teacher that's 70% "dog", 25% "wolf", 5% "cat" is teaching the student something about class structure that a one-hot "dog" label can't.

**3. Feature distillation.** Match the teacher's **internal representations** at one or more hidden layers. The student is forced to imitate the teacher's intermediate activations, typically with L1, L2, cosine, or KL-style losses, sometimes after a learned linear projection so dimensions align. Almost always layered *on top of* logits distillation rather than used alone. Most expressive, but also the most architectural plumbing — the student must have hooks at the right layers.

Across the three, the trend is clear: **more accuracy at the cost of more complexity** — both in the loss formulation and in keeping the teacher around during student training.

**Why does this work?** Smaller networks have enough capacity to fit the function the teacher learned; what they lack is the *inductive ease* of finding it from raw labels. The teacher's soft outputs and intermediate features provide a much smoother optimization target than one-hot ground truth, similar in spirit to label smoothing but informed by an actual model.

**Connection to scaling laws.** Scaling laws say bigger models reach lower loss but with diminishing returns; distillation is one of the main ways the field *operationalizes* big-model investments — train one giant teacher, distill many smaller deployable students from it. This complements **quantization** (precision compression) and **MoE** (capacity-vs-compute decoupling) as part of the standard efficiency toolkit. Where fine-tuning adapts a large model to a task, distillation produces a *new, smaller* model — the right tool when latency or memory, not just behavior, is the constraint.

**Exam-ready summary.** Direction: teacher to student. Response uses hard labels (cheap, lossy). Logits matches soft distributions via KL (carries uncertainty). Feature matches internal activations (richest). Trade: accuracy up, complexity up.

checks.q: In knowledge distillation, which direction does information flow?

options.text: Student to teacher
Teacher to student
Bidirectional, with alternating updates
Both models train independently, then are averaged

checks.explanation: Distillation always transfers the teacher's behavior into a smaller student — the student learns from the teacher's outputs or features.

checks.q: Why does logits (soft-label) distillation usually outperform response (hard-label) distillation?

options.text: It avoids using a softmax
Soft probability distributions encode the teacher's uncertainty and inter-class similarities (dark knowledge)
It allows the student to be larger than the teacher
It eliminates the need for any labeled data

checks.explanation: Hard labels collapse all of the teacher's relative confidence into a single argmax. Soft labels (especially with temperature) preserve the relative class structure the teacher learned.

checks.q: Which distillation flavor matches the teacher's internal hidden activations?

options.text: Response distillation
Logits distillation
Feature distillation
Quantization-aware distillation

checks.explanation: Feature distillation aligns intermediate representations using L1/L2/cosine/KL losses, usually layered on top of logits distillation.
